title: Cocadas
subtitle: Mexican coconut sweets with condensed milk
excerpt: Cocadas are a simple Mexican coconut sweet made by stirring desiccated coconut into condensed milk with a little egg, then spooning onto a tray and baking until the edges are golden and the centres are set but still chewy. They are barely a recipe (the whole process takes fifteen minutes) but the result is addictive: intensely sweet, coconutty, with a slight chew from the egg. They keep for several days in a tin.
sourceNotes: Cocadas are made throughout Mexico and the wider Caribbean, Central America, and South America, and reflect the Spanish colonial introduction of coconut across these regions. The Mexican version is typically small and round, baked or occasionally fried. They appear in Mexican cookbooks of the nineteenth century and are associated with street food vendors and home confectionery rather than formal patisserie. Similar coconut sweets made with condensed milk appear across Brazil (cocada) and Colombia.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 5 days at room temperature.

content.text: The mixture is just coconut, condensed milk, and egg stirred together. Spoon onto a lined tray in small mounds (they spread very little. The edges should be golden and the tops dry when they come out; the centres will still give slightly when pressed. They firm as they cool. Do not try to lift them off the tray while warm) the condensed milk is still tacky and they will tear.

Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with parchment. Combine 300 g desiccated coconut, 200 g condensed milk, 2 lightly beaten eggs, and 0.5 tsp vanilla extract in a bowl. Mix well until evenly combined. Drop rounded tablespoons of the mixture onto the lined trays, spaced about 3 cm apart. Press each mound gently to flatten slightly. Bake for 15 to 18 minutes until the edges are golden and the tops are dry. Cool completely on the tray (at least 15 minutes) before lifting with a palette knife.

items.problem: Cocadas stuck to the tray and fell apart
items.solution: They were lifted too soon. The condensed milk sets as it cools; warm from the oven it is still liquid and will pull apart. Cool fully on the parchment before attempting to remove.

items.problem: Very pale and soft with no colour
items.solution: Underbaked or the oven was too low. The edges should be genuinely golden, not just dry. If the tops are drying before the edges colour, move the tray to a higher shelf.
This is the character of the sweet — condensed milk is intensely sweet by design. For a less sweet version, use 150 g condensed milk and add 1 teaspoon of lemon juice to cut the sweetness.
